sourceDesc.p: This example has been created by retyping the first paragraphs of Annie Dillard's The Writing Life (1989), it is only intended to serve as an example of the visualisations.
Original text: Annie Dillard, The Writing Life (1989)

body.p:
When you write, you lay out a line of words. The line of words is a miner's pick, a woodcarver's gouge, a surgeon's probe. You wield it, and it digs a path you follow. Soon you find yourself deep in new territory. Is it a dead end, or have you located the real subject? You will know tomorrow, or this time next year.
You make the path boldly and follow it fearfully. You go where the path leads. At the end of the path, you find a box canyon. You hammer out reports, dispatch bulletins.
The pathwriting has changed, in your hands, and in a twinkling, from an expression of your notions to an epistemological tool. The new place interetsts you because you don't know itit is not clear. In your humility, you lay down the words carefully, awatching all the angles. Now the earlier writing looks soft and careless. EProcess is nothing; erase your rtracks. The apath is not the wrkork. I hope your pathtracks have grown over; I hope birds ate the crumbs; I hope you will toss it all and not look back.
